rewrites.newText: Decided by a single person
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misreads the 'uni-' prefix as 'one person decided'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: unanimous means everyone agrees, not that one person alone decided

rewrites.newText: Causing great surprise in the courtroom
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reads it as the reaction to the verdict rather than the agreement
rewrites.wrongnessReason: unanimous describes the jury all agreeing, not the courtroom's surprise

rewrites.newText: Warm and full of cheerful life
rewrites.wrongnessReason: bleak means cold and unwelcoming, the exact opposite of warm and cheerful

rewrites.newText: Enormous, grand and richly decorated inside
rewrites.targetsMisreading: surface assumption that a big house is grand
rewrites.wrongnessReason: bleak means bare and plain, nothing to do with grandeur

rewrites.newText: Moving at great speed throughout
rewrites.targetsMisreading: near-synonym wrong shade, reads persistent as fast
rewrites.wrongnessReason: persistent means refusing to give up, not moving quickly

rewrites.newText: Paying very close attention to detail
rewrites.wrongnessReason: persistent is about determination, not carefulness

rewrites.newText: Definitely false and containing an obvious mistake
rewrites.wrongnessReason: ambiguous means unclear or open to interpretation, not simply wrong

rewrites.newText: Perfectly clear and simple for anyone to follow
rewrites.wrongnessReason: ambiguous means unclear, the opposite of perfectly clear

rewrites.newText: Extremely fashionable and used by almost everyone right now
rewrites.targetsMisreading: antonym, reads it as still in wide use
rewrites.wrongnessReason: obsolete means no longer used, the opposite of currently popular

rewrites.newText: Recently invented and only just released onto the open market
rewrites.wrongnessReason: obsolete means out of date, not brand new

rewrites.newText: So costly that only a few wealthy people can afford it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: obsolete refers to being out of date and has nothing to do with price

rewrites.newText: Anxious and full of doubt
rewrites.wrongnessReason: confident means sure of oneself, the opposite of anxious and doubtful

rewrites.newText: Uncertain about his own ability
rewrites.wrongnessReason: confident means self-assured, not uncertain

rewrites.newText: Wishing serious harm on others
rewrites.wrongnessReason: benevolent means kind and generous, the opposite of wishing harm

rewrites.newText: Caring only about personal gain
rewrites.wrongnessReason: benevolent means generous towards others, not selfish

rewrites.newText: Ruling with great strength and authority
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confuses benevolent with a mighty ruler
rewrites.wrongnessReason: benevolent describes kindness, not power

rewrites.newText: Very unlikely ever to occur
rewrites.wrongnessReason: inevitable means certain to happen, not unlikely

rewrites.newText: Coming as a complete surprise
rewrites.wrongnessReason: an inevitable event is expected and certain, not a surprise

rewrites.newText: Beautiful and full of life
rewrites.wrongnessReason: desolate means bleak and empty, the opposite of beautiful and lively

rewrites.newText: Warm, cosy and full of visitors
rewrites.wrongnessReason: desolate means empty and barren, not warm and full of people

rewrites.newText: Lush, green and richly covered in crops
rewrites.wrongnessReason: desolate land is barren, the opposite of lush and fertile

rewrites.newText: Well known and celebrated for his earlier journeys
rewrites.wrongnessReason: audacious means boldly daring, not famous

rewrites.newText: Nervous and completely unwilling to take any real risks
rewrites.wrongnessReason: audacious means willing to take risks, the opposite of risk-averse

rewrites.newText: Extremely clever at outwitting each of the hero's allies
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malevolent means wishing to do evil, not merely being clever

rewrites.newText: Deeply lonely and bitter after long years spent in exile
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malevolent means wishing harm, nothing to do with loneliness

rewrites.newText: Strong enough in body to threaten and conquer the whole kingdom
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confuses evil intent with physical power
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malevolence is about evil intention, not physical strength

rewrites.newText: Worth almost no money
rewrites.wrongnessReason: precious means of great value, the opposite of nearly worthless

rewrites.newText: Extremely heavy and hard to lift
rewrites.wrongnessReason: precious refers to value, not physical weight

rewrites.newText: Feeling cross and irritable
rewrites.wrongnessReason: weary means tired, not angry

rewrites.newText: In need of a meal
rewrites.wrongnessReason: weary means tired, not hungry

rewrites.newText: Feeling nervous, frightened and on edge
rewrites.wrongnessReason: weary means tired, not scared

rewrites.newText: To keep something completely secret
rewrites.wrongnessReason: reveal means to make known, the opposite of keeping secret

rewrites.newText: To smash something into small pieces
rewrites.wrongnessReason: reveal means to disclose, not to destroy

rewrites.newText: Sprinted quickly across the ground
rewrites.targetsMisreading: near action but wrong, reads ventured as running
rewrites.wrongnessReason: ventured means dared to go cautiously, not to run

rewrites.newText: Soared high through the air
rewrites.wrongnessReason: ventured means dared to go on foot, not to fly

rewrites.newText: Tumbled suddenly down to the ground
rewrites.wrongnessReason: ventured means dared to go, not to fall

rewrites.newText: Hard to make any sense of
rewrites.wrongnessReason: magnificent means impressive, not confusing

rewrites.newText: Dull and far worse than expected
rewrites.wrongnessReason: magnificent means very impressive, the opposite of disappointing

rewrites.newText: A long and very tiring journey
rewrites.wrongnessReason: triumph means a great victory, not a journey

rewrites.newText: A crushing and humiliating defeat in battle
rewrites.wrongnessReason: triumph means victory, the opposite of a defeat

rewrites.newText: A sudden loud blast of trumpets and cheering
rewrites.wrongnessReason: triumph means a victory, not the sound of trumpets

rewrites.newText: Available in huge amounts
rewrites.wrongnessReason: scarce means in short supply, the opposite of plentiful

rewrites.newText: Huge in size and weight
rewrites.wrongnessReason: scarce refers to quantity being low, not to size

rewrites.newText: Bright, cheerful and full of joy
rewrites.wrongnessReason: solemn means serious and dignified, the opposite of cheerful

rewrites.newText: Amusing and quick to make jokes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: solemn means grave and serious, not funny

rewrites.newText: Noisy, rowdy and full of loud shouting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: solemn implies quiet dignity, not loud rowdiness

rewrites.newText: Struggling with very little money
rewrites.wrongnessReason: prosperous means wealthy, the opposite of poor

rewrites.newText: Isolated and with few friends
rewrites.wrongnessReason: prosperous means successful and wealthy, not lonely

rewrites.newText: Brief and quickly over
rewrites.wrongnessReason: tedious means long and boring, the opposite of brief

rewrites.newText: Scary and full of danger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: tedious means dull, not frightening

rewrites.newText: Thrilling and full of great excitement
rewrites.wrongnessReason: tedious means boring, the opposite of exciting

rewrites.newText: Be completely different from
rewrites.wrongnessReason: resemble means to look similar, the opposite of differing

rewrites.newText: Look up to with respect
rewrites.wrongnessReason: resemble means to look alike, not to admire

rewrites.newText: Unable to happen at all
rewrites.wrongnessReason: plausible means seeming probable, the opposite of impossible

rewrites.newText: Utterly foolish and impossible to believe
rewrites.wrongnessReason: plausible means believable, not foolish and unbelievable

rewrites.newText: Complicated and very hard to properly understand
rewrites.wrongnessReason: plausible means reasonable-sounding, not complicated

rewrites.newText: Making almost no sound at all
rewrites.wrongnessReason: conspicuous means clearly visible, nothing to do with sound

rewrites.newText: Marked with a few small scratches
rewrites.wrongnessReason: conspicuous means noticeable, not damaged

rewrites.newText: To lose bit by bit
rewrites.wrongnessReason: accumulate means to gather, the opposite of losing

rewrites.newText: To break down into many separate pieces
rewrites.wrongnessReason: accumulate means to build up, not break apart

rewrites.newText: To scatter something widely and thinly in every direction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: accumulate means to gather together, the opposite of scattering

rewrites.newText: A simple and obvious choice
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a dilemma is a difficult choice, the opposite of an easy one

rewrites.newText: A dull and repetitive daily routine
rewrites.wrongnessReason: a dilemma is a hard decision, not a routine

rewrites.newText: To put something firmly in
rewrites.wrongnessReason: omit means to leave out, the opposite of including

rewrites.newText: To explain something in full detail
rewrites.wrongnessReason: omit means to leave out, not to explain

rewrites.newText: To make something much better than before
rewrites.wrongnessReason: omit means to leave out, not to improve

rewrites.newText: Likely to cause serious harm
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal harmful-disease sense of the figurative word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: here infectious means spreading easily to others, not causing harm

rewrites.newText: Irritating and hard to put up with
rewrites.wrongnessReason: infectious enthusiasm spreads to others, it is not annoying

rewrites.newText: Clearly biased towards one particular side
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misreads the 'im-' prefix, thinks impartial means biased
rewrites.wrongnessReason: impartial means treating both sides equally, the opposite of biased

rewrites.newText: Extremely small and slight
rewrites.wrongnessReason: substantial means considerable in size, the opposite of tiny

rewrites.newText: Available completely free of charge
rewrites.wrongnessReason: substantial refers to size, not to cost

rewrites.newText: Damaged in one or two places
rewrites.wrongnessReason: substantial means large, nothing to do with being broken

rewrites.newText: To climb slowly and steadily upward
rewrites.wrongnessReason: plummet means to fall rapidly, the opposite of rising

rewrites.newText: To drift down gently and very slowly
rewrites.wrongnessReason: plummet means to drop rapidly, not to float gently

rewrites.newText: Left entirely up to personal choice
rewrites.wrongnessReason: compulsory means required, the opposite of optional

rewrites.newText: Carefully examine every part
rewrites.wrongnessReason: demolish means to knock down, not to examine

rewrites.newText: Fix and make it safe
rewrites.wrongnessReason: demolish means to destroy, the opposite of repairing

rewrites.newText: Cover the outside with fresh paint
rewrites.wrongnessReason: demolish means to knock down completely, not to paint over

rewrites.newText: Drooping and beginning to die off
rewrites.targetsMisreading: describes the roses' state rather than their smell
rewrites.wrongnessReason: fragrant refers to a pleasant smell, not to wilting

rewrites.newText: Covered all over in bright vivid colours
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong sense, sight instead of smell
rewrites.wrongnessReason: fragrant refers to smell, not to colour

rewrites.newText: A truthful and accurate account
rewrites.wrongnessReason: misleading means giving a false impression, the opposite of accurate

rewrites.newText: Lazy and unwilling to make effort
rewrites.wrongnessReason: diligent means hardworking, the opposite of lazy

rewrites.newText: Fast at working but careless and untidy
rewrites.wrongnessReason: diligent means careful and thorough, not careless

rewrites.newText: Very clever but likely to forget things easily
rewrites.wrongnessReason: diligent means hardworking, not clever

rewrites.newText: To thoroughly enjoy each and every moment
rewrites.wrongnessReason: endure means to suffer through, the opposite of enjoying

rewrites.newText: To escape from something completely and avoid it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: endure means to go through hardship, not to avoid it

rewrites.newText: Absolutely necessary and important
rewrites.wrongnessReason: superfluous means unnecessary, the opposite of essential

rewrites.newText: Timid and rather withdrawn
rewrites.wrongnessReason: candid means frank and open, the opposite of shy and withdrawn

rewrites.newText: Keeping thoughts carefully hidden away
rewrites.wrongnessReason: candid means openly honest, the opposite of secretive

rewrites.newText: Having a great deal of money
rewrites.wrongnessReason: candid means honest, nothing to do with wealth

rewrites.newText: Using very few short words
rewrites.wrongnessReason: verbose means using too many words, the opposite of brief

rewrites.newText: Speaking softly and hardly at all
rewrites.wrongnessReason: verbose means wordy, not quiet

rewrites.newText: Quick to forgive and forget any wrong
rewrites.wrongnessReason: vindictive means seeking revenge, the opposite of forgiving

rewrites.newText: Certain to succeed in the end
rewrites.wrongnessReason: futile means pointless and without effect, the opposite of successful

rewrites.newText: Very hard but still ultimately worthwhile
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reads futile as merely difficult yet effective
rewrites.wrongnessReason: futile means having no effect, so it cannot be worthwhile

rewrites.newText: Great fun and thoroughly enjoyable to do
rewrites.wrongnessReason: futile means pointless, not enjoyable

rewrites.newText: Having a great deal of wealth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confuses selfless generosity with being rich
rewrites.wrongnessReason: altruistic means selflessly caring for others, not being wealthy

rewrites.newText: Very powerful and strong in body
rewrites.wrongnessReason: altruistic describes selfless character, not physical strength

rewrites.newText: Rich, beautiful and highly poetic throughout
rewrites.wrongnessReason: austere means severe and plain, the opposite of rich and poetic

rewrites.newText: Passing quickly and soon forgotten
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reads acute as fleeting rather than intense
rewrites.wrongnessReason: here acute means very strong and intense, not brief

rewrites.newText: Faint, mild and hardly even noticeable
rewrites.wrongnessReason: acute means intense, the opposite of mild and faint

rewrites.newText: Warm, pleasant and thoroughly agreeable to feel
rewrites.wrongnessReason: an acute sense of injustice is intense and distressing, not pleasant

rewrites.newText: Light-hearted and amusing despite its very dark subject matter
rewrites.wrongnessReason: poignant means evoking sadness, the opposite of light-hearted

rewrites.newText: Dull and rather difficult to read all the way through
rewrites.wrongnessReason: poignant means emotionally moving, not boring

rewrites.newText: Warm, kind and genuinely friendly
rewrites.wrongnessReason: sardonic means grimly mocking, the opposite of warm and friendly

rewrites.newText: Full of unrealistic ideas that would never work
rewrites.wrongnessReason: pragmatic means practical and realistic, the opposite of unrealistic

rewrites.newText: Highly artistic and concerned mainly with beauty and appearance
rewrites.targetsMisreading: latches onto 'innovative' and reads it as artistic
rewrites.wrongnessReason: pragmatic means practical, not focused on artistic beauty

rewrites.newText: Costing a very large amount of money to build it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: pragmatic means practical, nothing to do with cost

rewrites.newText: Chatty and always talking
rewrites.wrongnessReason: reticent means reserved and quiet, the opposite of talkative

rewrites.newText: Warm, outgoing and very sociable
rewrites.wrongnessReason: reticent means reserved, not outgoing

rewrites.newText: Idle and producing very little
rewrites.wrongnessReason: prolific means producing a great deal, the opposite of unproductive

rewrites.newText: Recently built and in perfect, brand new condition
rewrites.wrongnessReason: dilapidated means in ruin and disrepair, the opposite of newly built

rewrites.newText: Beautifully decorated and carefully kept in excellent condition throughout
rewrites.wrongnessReason: dilapidated means run down, the opposite of well kept

rewrites.newText: Extremely large, with many wide and spacious rooms inside it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: dilapidated refers to disrepair, not to being large

rewrites.newText: Lasting only briefly and easily changed later on
rewrites.wrongnessReason: irrevocable means unable to be changed, the opposite of temporary

rewrites.newText: Extremely popular and welcomed warmly by all the people
rewrites.wrongnessReason: irrevocable means unchangeable, nothing to do with popularity

rewrites.newText: To copy the exact way that another person behaves
rewrites.wrongnessReason: placate means to soothe someone upset, not to copy them

rewrites.newText: Recently retired after a long and successful public career
rewrites.wrongnessReason: eminent means famous and respected, not retired

rewrites.newText: Nervously getting ready to give the important speech soon
rewrites.wrongnessReason: eminent means distinguished, not about to happen soon

rewrites.newText: Visiting here from a distant foreign university somewhere far overseas
rewrites.wrongnessReason: eminent means renowned, nothing to do with being foreign

rewrites.newText: A total lack of any agreement
rewrites.wrongnessReason: consensus means general agreement, the opposite of disagreement

rewrites.newText: A long and bitterly heated public argument
rewrites.targetsMisreading: seizes on the debate rather than the agreement
rewrites.wrongnessReason: consensus is the agreement reached, not the argument itself

rewrites.newText: A towering yellow crane lifted the heavy concrete slabs high onto the unfinished roof.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: uses 'crane' as the machinery noun, not the verb
rewrites.wrongnessReason: here crane is a construction machine, not the verb meaning to stretch forward to see

rewrites.newText: Very easy for anyone to see
rewrites.wrongnessReason: invisible means not able to be seen, the opposite of easy to see

rewrites.newText: Difficult but still just about possible to see
rewrites.wrongnessReason: invisible means impossible to see at all, not merely hard to see

rewrites.newText: Clearly seen by each and every single person
rewrites.wrongnessReason: invisible means unseen, the opposite of seen by everyone

rewrites.newText: To hide something away safely in a secret place
rewrites.wrongnessReason: transport means to carry across, not to hide

rewrites.newText: To break something completely apart into many separate small pieces
rewrites.wrongnessReason: transport means to carry from place to place, not to break apart

rewrites.newText: To say something only afterwards
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reads the 'pre-' prefix as 'after' instead of 'before'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: predict means to say before, not after

rewrites.newText: To work perfectly without any faults
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malfunction means to work badly or fail, the opposite of working perfectly

rewrites.newText: To work very loudly and make noise
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malfunction means to fail, nothing to do with loudness

rewrites.newText: To work slowly but still get things done
rewrites.wrongnessReason: malfunction means to fail or work badly, not simply to work slowly

rewrites.newText: To create trouble and stir up an argument
rewrites.wrongnessReason: break the ice means to start a conversation, not to cause trouble

rewrites.newText: To smash apart a thick sheet of frozen water
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of the figurative idiom
rewrites.wrongnessReason: break the ice is figurative, not literally smashing ice

rewrites.newText: Someone had attacked her with a real sharp weapon
rewrites.wrongnessReason: the dagger is a metaphor for emotional pain, not a literal attack
